summary: The notebook demonstrates an agentic pipeline that automates OpenAPI specification generation for CRMs by leveraging Google Gemini 1.5's 2-million-token context window. It compares traditional RAG with in-context retrieval, showing that direct context injection eliminates the need for vector databases. The workflow includes web crawling, context caching, extracting JSON/CURL examples, and using many-shot prompting via chat history to guide the model. Readers learn how to structure long-context prompts, manage costs with caching, and implement multi-agent refinement loops for API documentation.
competition_flow: Crawls CRM documentation, caches it via Gemini's context caching, extracts JSON/CURL examples, uses many-shot prompting in chat history, and runs an agentic pipeline to generate and refine OpenAPI specs from user prompts.
data_reading: Web crawling via urllib and BeautifulSoup to extract URLs, followed by aiohttp to asynchronously fetch and parse HTML body text; also loads pre-existing OpenAPI/JSON example files from Kaggle inputs.
data_processing: Deduplication of crawled URLs, filtering of LLM outputs to remove placeholders and empty responses, LLM-driven conversion of CURL commands to JSON responses, merging of multiple JSON scripts into a single corpus, and context caching for documentation storage.
insights: Gemini 1.5's massive context window can effectively replace traditional RAG pipelines for documentation-heavy tasks by enabling direct in-context retrieval.
Context caching drastically reduces latency and API costs when repeatedly querying large documentation corpora.
Passing many-shot examples via chat history instead of the system prompt prevents instruction dilution and maintains prompt coherence.
Multi-agent refinement loops (extraction, conversion, merging, correction) reliably produce syntactically valid OpenAPI specs without manual parsing.
critical_findings: In-context retrieval proved simpler, faster, and more accurate than a LlamaIndex-based RAG pipeline, eliminating the need for external vector stores.
Many-shot prompting with 10 large OpenAPI examples (~400k tokens) successfully guided the model without losing the core instruction, proving large-context few-shot works.
